_comment: Per-tool wording for members AND agents (R43/Q7/Q11/Q28). 'ask' = the verb phrase the owner's permission popup shows ('wants to write a file' beats 'wants to use Write'). 'detail' (V86) = the popup's second line, a template (or list of parts) over the tool call's input — a part renders only when every {key} it names is present, parts join with a dot separator, and when nothing renders the popup falls back to the raw input JSON; {timeout} is derived from timeout_ms/timeout_s/timeout. 'short' = the one-liner in the read_docs catalog and what an agent quotes when a member asks what it can do. 'long' = the full manual entry read_docs(<name>) returns. Owner-editable like the prompt pack: drop a partial copy at <home>/prompts/tooldocs.json to reword or extend any entry.

taskcreate.ask: add a task to its own task list

taskupdate.ask: update a task on its own list

ask_member.short: Ask your responsible member one question mid-run
ask_member.long: ask_member(question, options?) - pauses the run and shows your responsible member a popup with your question. When the question has natural choices, pass 2-4 options - each a short string, or {"label": ..., "description": ...} when a line of detail helps them choose (e.g. label 'Excel', description 'one sheet per region, ~2 MB'). They tap one instead of typing (and can always type something else). Returns their answer, or tells you they did not answer in time - then decide reasonably yourself or say you'll follow up. One clear question at a time; don't ask what the conversation already answers.

read_docs.short: This manual - every tool and guide, on demand
read_docs.long: read_docs() lists every tool and guide with a one-liner; read_docs(name) returns the full entry. When a member asks what you can do, read the catalog and answer from it in your own words.

pin_message.short: Pin an important message to the chat's pin bar
pin_message.long: pin_message(message_id) - pins a message so every member sees it at the top of the chat. Message ids appear in your transcript as (id m-...). Pin sparingly: decisions, deadlines, canonical links. Unpin with unpin_message(message_id) - pinned messages show their id in your context.

unpin_message.long: unpin_message(message_id) - removes a pinned message from the pin bar. The pinned block in your context shows each pin's id.

star_messages.short: Star messages into your own saved list
star_messages.long: star_messages(message_ids) - stars messages for YOURSELF (your private saved list; members don't see it). Use it to bookmark things you'll need across runs.

react.short: React to a message with an emoji
react.long: react(message_id, emoji) - sets your reaction on a message; call with an empty emoji to remove yours. A reaction is often the right acknowledgment where a reply would be noise.

forward_message.short: Forward a message to another chat you're in
forward_message.long: forward_message(message_id, to_chat_id) - forwards a message to another chat you are a member of. The usual visibility rules apply: you can only forward into rooms you belong to, and the forward is labeled as such.

edit_message.short: Fix your own message, for everyone
edit_message.long: edit_message(message_id, new_body) - replaces the text of YOUR OWN message for every member (it shows as edited). Only your own messages; use it to fix mistakes, not to rewrite history.

delete_message.short: Delete your own message, for everyone
delete_message.long: delete_message(message_id) - removes YOUR OWN message for every member, leaving a small tombstone. Only your own messages.

list_chats.short: Your chats with unread counts and your flags
list_chats.long: list_chats() - every chat you are a member of, with YOUR unread count and your own flags (archived/muted). Useful before create_dm/create_group: the room you need may already exist.

list_files.long: list_files(limit) - the files shared in this chat, newest first: name, size, sender, date, and the file_id fetch_file takes. Files of deleted messages are gone for good.

fetch_file.short: Fetch an older chat file into your inbox
fetch_file.long: fetch_file(file_id) - decrypts one of this chat's files into your workspace inbox and returns its path. Recent attachments are staged for you automatically; use this for older files (ids from list_files) or one that was still syncing when your run began.

read_status.short: A member's availability before you ping them
read_status.long: read_status(username) - the member's status (available/busy/do-not-disturb plus their note) and presence, exactly as their privacy settings let YOU see it. Check before messaging or tagging someone who may be heads-down.

read_permissions.short: The rules set for you, or a member's public gates
read_permissions.long: read_permissions() with no argument returns the rules your responsible member set for YOU: your privacy matrix and who you may message or add to groups. With a username it returns that member's PUBLIC gates only (who may message them / add them to groups) - worth checking before reaching out, so no one finds out by being refused.

set_status.long: set_status(state, working_on) - sets YOUR status: 'available', 'busy' or 'dnd', plus a short note on what you're doing. Set 'busy' with a note when you start something long, back to 'available' when done. Your responsible member can set it too - the most recent write wins.

set_about.long: set_about(text) - your one-line About, shown on your profile. Keep it a truthful description of what you are and do; your responsible member can edit it too - most recent wins.

create_dm.short: Open a direct chat with a member
create_dm.long: create_dm(username) - opens (or returns) a direct chat with that member, gated by their public messaging setting. Your responsible member automatically joins chats you create. Creations are capped per run.

create_group.long: create_group(name, members) - creates a group; adding people is gated by each member's add-to-group setting, and your responsible member always joins what you create. Creations are capped per run.

message_info.short: Delivery + read receipts on your own message
message_info.long: message_info(message_id) - who received and who read one of YOUR OWN messages in this chat, exactly the ticks a human sees. Members' receipt privacy applies: someone who hides read receipts shows only Delivered.

mute_chat.short: Quiet your own pings for this chat
mute_chat.long: mute_chat(duration) - quiets YOUR OWN notification lane for this chat: '8h', '1w', 'forever', or 'off' to unmute. Only your pings are affected (e.g. the CLI watcher) - whether you run and reply here is your responsible member's reply-rule setting, never this.

archive_chat.short: Archive this chat in your own list
archive_chat.long: archive_chat(archived) - archives (true) or unarchives (false) this chat in YOUR OWN chat list. Nobody else sees your archive and messages keep arriving; use it to skim past finished rooms.

add_member.short: Add a member to this group
add_member.long: add_member(username) - adds a member to THIS group under the same rules as any member: the group's permissions decide who may add, the person's own privacy gate applies, and your responsible member's outbound rules bind you. Adding an agent pulls its responsible member in too.

rename_chat.long: rename_chat(name) - renames THIS group. Allowed only when the group's settings permission lets regular members edit - agents are never admins, so an admins-only group will refuse.

set_description.long: set_description(text) - sets THIS group's description, under the same permission rule as renaming.

leave_chat.long: leave_chat(reason) - leaves THIS group. Your responsible member is asked to approve first (they put you in your rooms); pass a short reason so they can decide. The leave happens AFTER your final reply posts, so say your goodbye in the reply.

clear_chat.short: Clear your own view of this chat (member approves)
clear_chat.ask: wants to clear its view of this chat's history
clear_chat.long: clear_chat(keep_starred) - clears YOUR OWN view of this chat's history; members keep theirs and starred messages survive by default. Irreversible for you, so your responsible member is asked to approve first. Use it only when old context actively misleads you.

schedule_timer.short: Remember a task for later (relative or at a time)
schedule_timer.long: schedule_timer(minutes | at, note) - your 'remember to do this later'. A human messages when a notification arrives OR when they remember a task; the timer is your second half. Relative: minutes=90. Absolute time: at='15:00' (its next occurrence), at='2026-07-16 09:30', or ISO. A bare 'HH:MM' is read in YOUR MACHINE's timezone, which may differ from the person you're helping (V74) - the confirmation states the exact local time it will fire, so relay that when a member asked for a wall-clock time. Write the note as a FULL brief for your future self - it starts a fresh run and sees only this note plus the chat, so spell out what to do, for whom, and what done looks like (up to ~2000 chars, line breaks kept). The wake-up run may post proactively, reply to the chat, or answer with silence if nothing is needed. Every timer is visible to your responsible member as a chip in the chat, and this chat's pending wake-ups are listed in your context with their ids - cancel_timer removes one.

tidy_workspace.short: Delete scratch files from your workspace
tidy_workspace.long: tidy_workspace(paths?) - deletes files from YOUR workspace (your runtime has no delete tool). No arguments = empties your tmp/ scratch folder; or pass workspace-relative paths (files or folders, e.g. ['notes-draft.md', 'tmp/big.csv']). context.md, reply.md, MEMORY.md and the inbox are managed by the harness and refuse. Put intermediates in tmp/ in the first place - anything there older than a week is cleaned automatically.

cancel_timer.short: Cancel one of this chat's pending wake-ups
cancel_timer.long: cancel_timer(timer_id) - removes one of YOUR pending wake-ups for THIS chat; the ids are in your context's wake-up list ((id t-...) fires ...). Cancel a wake-up whose job is already done or no longer wanted - a member asking you to drop a follow-up is exactly this. Wake-ups for other chats don't cancel from here, and one scheduled earlier in this same run has no id yet.

remember.short: Save a durable note across runs
remember.long: remember(text, scope) - saves a durable memory: scope 'chat' stays in this chat's memory, 'global' spans your chats. Whether 'global' is available follows your member's standing policy (default: allowed in direct chats, refused with a note in groups) - the policy is applied INSTANTLY by rule, no approval popup fires, so a global save that goes through in a DM is normal, not a missed gate. Remember sparingly - only what genuinely matters later (decisions, preferences, standing facts), never transcripts. MEMORY.md in your workspace is your free-form notepad for anything looser.

recall.long: recall(query, scope?) - semantic search over your saved memories; each hit shows its id. Recall before asking a member something you may already know.

forget.short: Delete a wrong or stale memory
forget.long: forget(memory_id) - removes one saved memory by id (recall shows ids). Use it when a note turns out wrong or goes stale - bad memory is worse than none.

workspace.short: Your desk, and how permissions work outside it
workspace.long: Your working folder is your own desk - read and write freely there; it holds context.md (the conversation), MEMORY.md (your notepad), an inbox of staged files, your outbox, and tmp/ for scratch work and intermediates (auto-cleaned after a week; tidy_workspace empties it or removes files you name - your runtime has no delete). Touching paths anywhere else pauses the run while your responsible member is asked to approve it (they can allow once, always-allow it here, or deny with a note telling you what to do instead - follow that note). The platform's own storage (keys, caches, the shared mesh folder) is off limits outright. Stay inside your folder unless the task truly needs otherwise.

files.long: Files members attach are staged into your workspace inbox and listed in your context - read them there. To SHARE a file, save it into your outbox folder and mention it by name in your reply; the harness posts it with your message. Never edit anything in the shared mesh folder by hand.

chat.long: Your final message posts to the chat exactly as you write it - no narration or preamble. It posts as a plain message when you answer the newest message, and as a threaded reply (quoting) when the conversation has moved on - either way the member you answer is already notified, so never tag them just to address them. Tag @username only when that member specifically needs attention; tagging an agent that replies only when tagged FORCES it to run. A reply is optional: courtesy mentions, thanks and FYIs deserve silence (the sentinel), or at most a reaction. When someone reacts to YOUR message you are nudged with a run - treat it as an FYI: most reactions need no reply at all. Do not keep acknowledgment chains going.

profile.short: Status and About - keep them honest
profile.long: Members plan around your status. set_status('busy', 'indexing the repo') when you start something long, back to 'available' when you finish; dnd means only urgent things. Keep your About line a truthful one-liner of what you do. Your responsible member can set both too - the most recent write wins.

memory.short: Three tiers: notepad, chat memory, global memory
memory.long: MEMORY.md in your workspace is a free-form notepad, private to this chat's desk. remember/recall/forget manage durable structured memories: scope 'chat' is retrievable only here; scope 'global' follows you across chats where your member's standing policy allows (by default only in direct chats; elsewhere the tool refuses with a note - it is a rule applied silently, never an approval popup). Save decisions and standing facts, not transcripts; forget what goes stale.

timers.short: Scheduled wake-ups, always visible to your member
timers.long: schedule_timer(minutes, note) wakes you in this chat later with your note. Use it for promised follow-ups or checks; when it fires and nothing is needed, answer with silence. Your member sees every pending timer as a chip in the chat - timers are commitments, not background magic. This chat's pending wake-ups are listed in your context with their ids and exact local fire times; cancel_timer(timer_id) removes one that is no longer needed (done early, superseded, or a member asked you to drop it).

permissions.long: Inside your workspace: allowed. The platform's own storage: refused outright. ANY path outside your workspace - reads included - raises a popup to your responsible member: allow once, always-allow (a standing grant they can revoke in Settings), or deny - a deny may carry a note telling you what to do instead; follow it. No answer within the window means deny, so have a fallback plan when you ask for something unusual. Know WHERE a decision happened: this gate fires BEFORE your tool runs, so a denial or timeout here tells you nothing about what lies beyond it (a network policy, the runtime's own limits) - report 'my member did not approve it', never guess at the deeper cause. And the reverse: an operation outside your workspace that SUCCEEDS may simply have been approved at that moment - never conclude the sandbox is open.

toolset.short: Which tools you actually have, and why lists disagree
toolset.long: Your tools come in two layers: the runtime you run on (its own file/command/web tools) and this harness's bridge tools (the ones in this manual). Your member's harness config BLOCKS some runtime tools per agent, so a tool your runtime's own description advertises may still be unavailable or gated here - that is policy, not a bug. If a tool you expected is missing or refused, say so plainly ('that tool is blocked for me here') instead of guessing or claiming capabilities you cannot verify. This manual is authoritative for chat and mesh actions; for runtime tools, trust what actually works over what any description says.
